originalTitle: SpaceX stock has cooled. Hiring for jobs in the space economy hasn't
summary: The SpaceX IPO euphoria is over, but the bullish trend in space economy jobs remains in place within a labor market where many other sectors have slowed hiring.
content:
Clearly, there's money to be made in space. SpaceX's historic initial public offering minted a $2 trillion company. And even as shares in Elon Musk's company come down from their IPO high , there remains an underlying boom in the space economy that is creating a new job market for Americans.

The space economy is growing domestically and around the globe, at an annual rate of 9% , according to the World Economic Forum. In the U.S., gross output in the space economy increased by nearly $51.5 billion from 2012 to 2023. The sector's total value reached an all-time high of $613 billion in Q2 2025, according to the Space Foundation.

As the space economy grows, it is spurring national job creation. In the private sector alone, over 373,000 employees work space-sector jobs, according to the most recent estimates from the Department of Commerce Bureau of Economic Analysis. That remains a small fraction of the total U.S. private-sector workforce, but one that is growing rapidly. Space-sector employment increased by 27% in the decade through 2024, far outpacing total private-sector employment growth at 14%, and with its rate of growth accelerating in the more recent years. From 2019 to 2024 alone, the space economy's job market grew by 18%.

Young workers in particular have played a major role in this growth. According to the U.S. Census Bureau , nearly half of the new jobs being added to the space economy are filled by workers under the age of 35, accounting for a 3% total increase in young workers' share of its workforce from 2014 to 2024. Across most major lines of work in the space sector, there has been an increase in the share of young workers employed. That means the sector isn't just growing, but also defying the trend of decreasing young worker share seen throughout other Census-surveyed sectors, including professional services and media.

Dean Boerner, a lead data scientist at Revelio Labs, found in his recent research looking across tens of thousands of postings from hundreds of space sector companies that the industry is significantly outperforming the broader labor market in providing current career opportunities.

"Active postings by companies operating within the space economy are up more than 40% year-over-year as of this month (and have generally been elevated this entire year, compared to 2025)," Boerner said. "U.S. postings overall are down about 5%, making the rise in opportunities within aerospace particularly striking," he added.

Compensation for aerospace-centered work is attractive. The private space sector boasts a combined annual payroll of around $57.9 billion , with median annual salaries varying by occupation, but typically within the range of $100,000 to $135,000 . Base salaries, however, are only one part of the employee compensation packages seen in the private sector. Large private space market employers often offer stock options, giving employees the opportunity to get in early on what could become a major publicly-traded company. In the case of SpaceX's historic IPO, thousands of current and former employees became millionaires overnight thanks to their pre-owned shares. Over 100 saw a newfound net worth of over $1 billion.

"This job market is competitive, often with thousands of applications for each entry-level role," said Dave Baldwin, director of talent acquisition at Firefly Aerospace , which went public last August.

And yet, thousands of positions at these companies remain unfilled on any given day. In fact, despite the attractive roles and seemingly promising upward trends in an increasingly lucrative field, employment in the space economy has largely failed to keep pace with industry scaling. Space sector companies of all varieties, in recent years, have seen prolonged hiring periods, high employee turnover rates, and persistent labor shortages. One reason is that the work relies heavily on highly skilled labor, disproportionately within the realm of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM).

Recent estimates indicate that over half of private-sector space economy jobs "require STEM skills," approximately double the national average. STEM skills, as important as they are, pose a real hurdle for firms looking to recruit and retain new talent. Only about a quarter of the American workforce has formal STEM training, a far smaller fraction of which has the specific vocational background needed in aerospace production. For employers building out their presence in the space economy, this means continually competing for the select pool of workers who possess the skillsets needed to sustain current operations and long-term growth.

SpaceX, in its own S-1 filing ahead of its IPO, acknowledged this issue as a potential risk for investors, stating: "We depend on our ability to recruit and retain employees who have advanced engineering and technical skills, and intense competition for such employees may increase costs and affect our ability to meet development and production timelines."

"The current tight labor market has adversely impacted our ability to recruit qualified personnel, including engineers, particularly with respect to our AI segment," the filing noted, underscoring the challenges posed by rapid space economy expansion.

Revelio Labs' data shows the magnitude of the issue, with the 45% delta in active postings between the sector and the rest of the economy (40% growth in postings for space jobs and 5% decline for all U.S. jobs).

Several active, high-profile employers in the aerospace sector are at the forefront of hiring struggles. Lockheed Martin has the second-most open postings among all employers, with 10,614, a figure that has increased by over 5,000 from this time last year. RTX Corp leads all employers with 12,871 openings globally. According to Boerner, the most in-demand roles are, in order, Safety Engineer, Information Security, Integration Engineer, Reliability Engineer, and Hardware Engineer, with each role requiring at
chineseTitle: SpaceX 的股价已经降温。太空经济领域的就业岗位尚未招聘
chineseSummary: SpaceX IPO 的狂热已经结束，但在许多其他行业放缓招聘的劳动力市场中，太空经济就业的看涨趋势仍然存在。
chineseContent:
显然，太空领域可以赚钱。 SpaceX 历史性的首次公开募股造就了一家价值 2 万亿美元的公司。即使埃隆·马斯克公司的股价从首次公开募股的高点回落，太空经济的潜在繁荣仍然存在，正在为美国人创造新的就业市场。

根据世界经济论坛的数据，太空经济在国内和全球范围内以每年 9% 的速度增长。在美国，从 2012 年到 2023 年，太空经济的总产值增加了近 515 亿美元。根据太空基金会的数据，该行业的总价值在 2025 年第二季度达到了 6130 亿美元的历史新高。

随着太空经济的增长，它正在刺激国家创造就业机会。根据美国商务部经济分析局的最新估计，仅在私营部门，就有超过 373,000 名员工从事太空领域的工作。这仍然只占美国私营部门劳动力总数的一小部分，但正在迅速增长。截至 2024 年的十年间，航天部门的就业人数增长了 27%，远远超过私营部门 14% 的总就业增长率，并且近年来增速不断加快。仅从 2019 年到 2024 年，太空经济的就业市场就增长了 18%。

尤其是年轻工人在这一增长中发挥了重要作用。根据美国人口普查局的数据，太空经济新增就业岗位中近一半由 35 岁以下的工人担任，从 2014 年到 2024 年，年轻工人占劳动力的比例总共增加了 3%。在航天领域的大多数主要工作领域，年轻工人的就业比例都在增加。这意味着该行业不仅在增长，而且还与其他接受人口普查调查的行业（包括专业服务和媒体）中年轻工人比例下降的趋势相悖。

Revelio Labs 的首席数据科学家 Dean Boerner 在最近的研究中发现，在查看数百家航天行业公司的数万条帖子时，该行业在提供当前职业机会方面明显优于更广泛的劳动力市场。

Boerner 表示：“截至本月，太空经济领域的公司的活跃职位数量同比增长了 40% 以上（与 2025 年相比，今年全年的活跃职位数量总体有所上升）。”他补充道：“美国的职位总数下降了约 5%，这使得航空航天领域的就业机会增长尤其引人注目。”

以航空航天为中心的工作的报酬很有吸引力。私营航天部门的年薪总额约为 579 亿美元，年薪中位数因职业而异，但通常在 100,000 美元至 135,000 美元之间。然而，基本工资只是私营部门员工薪酬方案的一部分。大型私人太空市场雇主通常会提供股票期权，让员工有机会尽早加入可能成为大型上市公司的公司。在 SpaceX 历史性的 IPO 中，数千名现任和前任员工凭借二手股票一夜之间成为百万富翁。超过 100 人的新净资产超过 10 亿美元。

去年八月上市的 Firefly Aerospace 人才招聘总监戴夫·鲍德温 (Dave Baldwin) 表示：“这个就业市场竞争激烈，每个初级职位往往都会有数千份申请。”

然而，这些公司每天都有数千个职位空缺。事实上，尽管在这个利润日益丰厚的领域中扮演着有吸引力的角色并且看似有希望的上升趋势，但太空经济的就业在很大程度上未能跟上行业规模的增长。近年来，各种航天领域的公司都出现了招聘周期延长、员工流动率高以及持续的劳动力短缺等问题。原因之一是这项工作严重依赖高技能劳动力，尤其是在科学、技术、工程和数学 (STEM) 领域。

最近的估计表明，超过一半的私营部门太空经济工作“需要 STEM 技能”，大约是全国平均水平的两倍。 STEM 技能虽然很重要，但对于寻求招聘和留住新人才的公司来说却构成了真正的障碍。只有大约四分之一的美国劳动力接受过正规的 STEM 培训，其中拥有航空航天生产所需的特定职业背景的人要少得多。对于在太空经济中拓展业务的雇主来说，这意味着不断争夺拥有维持当前运营和长期增长所需技能的精选工人。

SpaceX 在 IPO 前的 S-1 文件中承认这一问题对投资者来说是一个潜在风险，并表示：“我们依赖于我们招募和留住拥有先进工程和技术技能的员工的能力，对此类员工的激烈竞争可能会增加成本并影响我们满足开发和生产时间表的能力。”

该文件指出：“当前紧张的劳动力市场对我们招聘包括工程师在内的合格人员的能力产生了不利影响，特别是在我们的人工智能领域。”该文件强调了太空经济快速扩张带来的挑战。

Revelio Labs 的数据显示了问题的严重性，该行业与其他经济部门之间的活跃职位数量相差 45%（太空职位职位数量增长 40%，美国所有职位职位数量下降 5%）。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于财经市场信号，适合用于观察利率、汇率、股债商品、金融机构和宏观政策变化。
